coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of Rutin loaded on magnetic graphene quantum dot nano career with metals (Ag, Zn, Mg, Co, and Fe) and study of antioxidant and electrochemical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Salehpour, Pooneh
Abri, Abdolreza
coredata.dc:description: Rutin, is one of the flavonoids, and has high antioxidant properties, but is limited due to low solubility in aqueous solutions. To increase the antioxidant performance and permeability, the synthesis of magnetic graphene quantum dot metal nanocomposites and the antioxidant activity of the prepared nanocomposites have been studied. Rutin was treated with metal magnetic nanocomposites, GQD-MFe2O4, that included metals like Silver, Magnesium, Zinc, Cobalt, and Iron. The metal nanocomposites reacted with Rutin to obtain Rutin-bearing magnetic nanocomposites. The GQD-MFe2O4 nanocomposites were synthesized by common hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and subsequent the addition of iron nitrate and metal nitrate (M= Ag, Mg, Co, Zn, Fe) to the media. The scavenging test of free radical activity and DPPH test of prepared nanomagnetic composites show good antioxidant activity. The results show that the derivatives of magnetic nanocomposites have different antioxidant activities compared to Rutin as ordered: GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru<GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru<Rutin<AGQD-MgFe2O4-Ru<GQD-Fe3O4-Ru<GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru.The structure and morphology of magnetic graphene quantum dots metal composites and related Rutin loaded nanocomposites have been tested and proved by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC), UV-Visible, and Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) techniques. The morphology of nanocomposites shows particles less than 60 nm. The CV plot of nanocomposites represents the quasi-reversible performance of the nanocomposites. The results show that the derivatives of magnetic nanocomposites have different antioxidant activities compared to Rutin.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of Rutin loaded on magnetic graphene quantum dot nano career with metals (Ag, Zn, Mg, Co, and Fe) and study of antioxidant and electrochemical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, P.O. Box 5375171379, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz, Postcode: 5375171379, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rutin, is one of the flavonoids, and has high antioxidant properties, but is limited due to low solubility in aqueous solutions. To increase the antioxidant performance and permeability, the synthesis of magnetic graphene quantum dot metal nanocomposites and the antioxidant activity of the prepared nanocomposites have been studied. Rutin was treated with metal magnetic nanocomposites, GQD-MFe2O4, that included metals like Silver, Magnesium, Zinc, Cobalt, and Iron. The metal nanocomposites reacted with Rutin to obtain Rutin-bearing magnetic nanocomposites. The GQD-MFe2O4 nanocomposites were synthesized by common hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and subsequent the addition of iron nitrate and metal nitrate (M= Ag, Mg, Co, Zn, Fe) to the media. The scavenging test of free radical activity and DPPH test of prepared nanomagnetic composites show good antioxidant activity. The results show that the derivatives of magnetic nanocomposites have different antioxidant activities compared to Rutin as ordered: GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru<GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru<Rutin<AGQD-MgFe2O4-Ru<GQD-Fe3O4-Ru<GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru.The structure and morphology of magnetic graphene quantum dots metal composites and related Rutin loaded nanocomposites have been tested and proved by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC), UV-Visible, and Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) techniques. The morphology of nanocomposites shows particles less than 60 nm. The CV plot of nanocomposites represents the quasi-reversible performance of the nanocomposites. The results show that the derivatives of magnetic nanocomposites have different antioxidant activities compared to Rutin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Flavonoids are one of the essential active ingredient classes of plants that have a role in a bioactive product. Rutin, as one of the flavones, with powerful antioxidant properties, was known as a medicinal flavonoid belonging to the natural antioxidant class [1–3]. It is citrus flavonoid glycoside also called rutoside, quercetin-3-Rutinoside, and sophora. It consists of quercetin with the hydroxyl group at the position of C-3 substituted with glucose and rhamnose sugar groups. The chemical structure of Rutin is a glycoside comprising flavonol aglycone quercetin combined with disaccharide Rutinose [4–6]. Rutin, is one of the good chelating agents, with multiple hydroxyl groups that can be reacted with ion metals containing Zr4+, MO2+, Cu2+, etc. With a stable structural composition, Rutin has the potential to inhibit free radicals from non-toxic products without changing its original structure [7–12]. The thermal properties and antioxidant activity of these metal complexes were studied and reported in the literature [13,14]. As an exogenous antioxidant, it has bioactivity properties and acts as radical scavengers, hydrogen donors, electron donors, peroxide decomposers, enzyme inhibition, and synergic and metal agents [15,16]. Because of its pharmacological properties, which consist of anti-oxidative, anti-allergic, anti-inflammatory, and anti-cancer properties it has been widely studied over the decades [17–21]. Because of poor absorption and limited solubility in water (0.125 g/L), bioavailability was decreased [22]. Free radicals are known to be responsible for the primary cause of diseases such as cancer, allergy, and autoimmune diseases. Antioxidants such as flavonoids are reducing agents for scavenging free radicals and protect cells from undergoing degeneration [23].

There are many different technics could be used to increase the bioavailability of poorly soluble flavonoids, such as ionized salts addition, solid dispersions, micro ionization, nanospheres, liposomes, chelating metal complexes, cyclodextrin complexes, nanostructured systems, nanocrystal technology, polymers loading to flavonoid and micro emulation. However, there are some limitations to these strategies such as drug-loading capacity, toxicity, biodegradability, large dosages, and environmental considerations and stability [24–32]. One of the modern strategies for increasing bioactivity is preparing nanomaterials with suitable-defined size distributions that can release their active ingredients loaded in the target [33–35]. Nanomaterials such as graphene have been widely used in many fields of biomedicine studies. The application of graphene in the medical field could improve treatment and prevention techniques. Graphene derivatives such as graphene quantum dots, due to their various physicochemical properties, including nanoscale size, high surface area, and electrical charge, could be used as advanced multifunctional materials in biotechnology and medicine for cancer treatment, drug delivery, and cellular imaging [36–39]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) contain one or a few layers of graphene that have a smaller size than 100 nm. They have a large surface area and can be dispersed easily in water due to functional groups at the edges. There are many methods reported for preparing GQDs, some of these methods need to perform several steps and post-treatment with surface passivating agents to reach good water solubility and luminescence properties [40–42]. Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots were prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid. The pyrolysis of citric acid was carried out at different pHs to obtain the GQDs that were studied in detail [43].

In nanoscience, graphene quantum dots with zero-dimensional graphene nanoparticles have unique properties due to the edge effects, and it is a suitable candidate for drug delivery with fluorescence imaging of cellular structures [43].

Magnetic nanoparticles with magnetic resonance imaging immobilized on GQDs offer a multifunctional platform for effective drug delivery and monitoring by magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging [44,45]. Here in we report the synthesis and preparation of some magnetic nano-magnetic composites of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and MFe2O4 (M = Ni, Co, Zn, Ag, Mg) with intrinsic photo-luminescent and magnetic properties were produced by a convenient co-precipitation method. The prepared magnetic nanocomposites can be recovered by a magnetic field and with good stability. The magnetic GQDs were treated with Rutin [46–48].

The graphene compounds such as graphene quantum dots known as a good carrier for biological actives compounds. Herein, we report the synthesis and design of first new magnetic graphene quantum dot loaded with Rutin (GQD-MFe2O4-Ru) as a carrier for biological active Rutin with increased antioxidant activity, good electrochemical performance, easy to work with various metals.

Rutin is provided by Nutragreenlife biotechnology (98%). Citric acid monohydrate, Ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)3.9H2O) Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2.2H2O), Cobalt Nitrate (Co(NO3)2.6H2O), , Silver nitrate (Ag (NO3), Ferric chloride (FeCl3.6H2O), Magnesium Nitrate (Mg(NO3)2.6H2O), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Merck. Other chemicals and solvents such as ethanol, and methylene chloride were of analytical grade and used as received without more purification.

The FT-IR spectra of synthesized materials were recorded on a Bruker-Vector 22 spectrometer. Elemental analyses were carried out with an Elementar Vario EL III instrument. DCS analysis was carried out by a DSC 822 from Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland, under nitrogen at atmospheric pressure with the rate of 10 ºC/min. The morphologies and microstructures of the prepared samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) using an FE-SEM (TESCAN MIRA3) instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of samples were carried out by using a Bruker AXS model D8 Advance diffractometer, using Cu-Kα radiation (0.1542 nm), with the Bragg angle ranging from 2° to 70° at 40 kV and 25 °C. The electrochemical performances, cyclic voltammetry (CV), of Rutin, carried out in dry acetonitrile solutions in the presence of lithium perchlorate (0.1 M) a supporting electrolyte at room temperature. The electrochemical performance of synthesized magnetic graphene quantum dot nanocomposites was carried out in H2SO4 0.1 M and naphion /isopropyl alcohol solution cast on a glassy carbon electrode. Autolab (PGASTAT 30) Potentiostat/Galvanostat equipped with a standard three-electrode system was used in electrochemical experiments. A silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrode and a platinum electrode were used as the reference. The counter electrodes and a glass carbon were used as the working electrode, respectively. UV–Visible spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer 250 spectrometer.

Graphene Quantum dots solution was prepared by the direct pyrolysis of citric acid. Citric acid monohydrate 2 gr was put into a 5 mL round-bottom glass vessel and heated up to 200°C [42], after 35 min, the color changed to orange, indicating the formation of GODs. Then, the orange liquid was added dropwise to 100 mL of primary ethanol (10 mg/mL NaOH) under vigorous stirring.

Spinel ferrites NPs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method [43]. Fe(NO3)3.9H2O 0.01 mol (4.03 gr), and 0.005 mol (Zn(NO3)2.2H2O (1.127 gr) or Co(NO3)2.6H2O (1.455 gr), or Mg(NO3)2.6H2O (1.282 gr), or 2AgNO3 ( 1.698 gr), or FeCl3.6H2O ( 1.355 gr)) were dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water and stirred vigorously for 30 min at room temperature under nitrogen atmosphere. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 11 by dropwise adding 1 M NaOH solution after stirring for 6 h. The solution was transferred into a flask and heated to 180°C for 12 h under reflux conditions. The precipitates were washed with distilled water and ethanol (2 *100 mL) by employing an external magnetic field and then dried at 80°C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General producer for preparation of MFe2O4/GQD (M=Co, Ag, Mg, Zn, Fe) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, MFe2O4 (500 mg) was dispersed in 25 mL of the GQD solution and then ultra-sonicated for 10 min. Then, the mixture was refluxed under a nitrogen atmosphere at a temperature of 70 °C and stirred vigorously for two h. Finally, the precipitation was washed under an external magnetic field with ethanol several times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The reaction scheme is shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General producer for preparation of Rutin-MFe2O4/GQD (M=Co, Ag, Mg, Zn, Fe) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: MFe2O4/GQD (0.1 g) (M=Co, Ag, Mg, Zn, Fe) nanocomposites were dispersed in 20 mL of dichloromethane for 20 min. The mixture was stirred under nitrogen gas at a temperature of 30 °C and added 0.1 g Rutin, stirred vigorously for four h. The precipitations were washed under an external magnetic field with ethanol several times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Scheme 1.

DPPH (49 mg) was dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol 96% and the 0.1 mL of DPPH solution was mixed with various volumes of 3 mg/mL the Rutin/ethanol solution (0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 20 mL). Ethanol 96% (5 mL) was considered as an ethanol blank. DPPH solution (0.1 mL) was added to 5 mL ethanol and intended as a reference solution. After one h incubation at room temperature and protection from light, the absorbance of the mixture samples was measured using a spectrophotometer at 517 nm against ethanol blank. The radical scavenging activity of the samples was evaluated by percentage compared with a reference sample. The percentage radical scavenging activity (RSA) was calculated using the following Equation 1:
%RSA = [(A0-A1)/A0] × 100
Where A0 is the absorbance of the reference solution, and A1 is the absorbance of samples after one h.

Thanks to the exciting properties of nanocarbon materials and the continuation of our research, we decided to study and preparation of some quantum dot (QD) derivatives with various metals. These prepared quantum dot metals chelated to the Rutin, an interesting biological compound.

By exploring the literature, the complexation of Rutin occurred in two different sites on the Rutin structure. As described in Scheme 1, site A and B in the Rutin structure are possible interaction position with metals like Ni, V, Zn, Co, Mg, Ag, and other metals.
The possible mechanism for the formation of metal oxide is represented below:
Initially, the nano iron oxide, γ-Fe2O3, was formed, and subsequently, the second metal oxide that formed in situ was inserted into the iron oxide matrix.

Fe(NO3)30.9 H2O → Fe(NO3)3 + 9 H2O.
M(NO3)2.6 H2O → M(NO3)2 + 6H2O
2Fe(NO3)3 → γ-Fe2O3 + NOx
M(NO3)2 → MO + NOx
γ-Fe2O3 → α-Fe2O3
α-Fe2O3 + MO → MFe2O4

The GQDs were prepared by the direct pyrolysis of citric acid, and the as-synthesized GQDs were modified by the metals, MFe2O4, such as Zn, Co, Ag, and Mg via hydrothermal one-step co-precipitation method. For GQDs nanocomposite metal, ZnFe2O4, the GQDs solution was hydrothermally treated with Zn(NO3)2 and FeCl3 at 80 °C and ethanol-water solvent. In the same method, Co, Mg, Ag, and Fe composite with GQDs were successfully prepared. The prepared nanomagnetic metal composites were dispersed in dichloromethane solvent by ultrasonic waves and treated with the Rutin solution under reflux conditions. The as-prepared nanomagnetic Rutin composites, (GQD-MFe2O4-Ru), were centrifuged and dried in a vacuum for two h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rutin-bearing magnetic nanocomposites characterization. FT-IR and DSC Studies
ce:section.ce:para: For further investigation of GQDs metal nanocomposite, the FT-IR experiment was carried out and represented in Fig. 2. The Rutin spectrum is depicted in Fig. 2 e. The strong bond peaks that can be seen at 3423 and 3320 cm−1 belong to OH stretching and the observed peaks at 2937, 2856, and 3091 cm−1 are attributed to the C-H stretching of aliphatic and aromatics, respectively. The CO vibration appeared at 1655 cm−1, and the double bond stretching vibration appeared at 1602, 1456 cm−1.

In Fig. 2 a, the GQD-Fe3O4 spectrum was depicted. The OH stretching vibration can be seen at 3421 cm−1, and the aliphatic C–H vibration presented at 2923, 2853 cm−1. The appeared peak at 1621 cm−1 belongs to the CO stretching vibration that can be resulted from a carboxylic acid group in GQD. The double bond stretching vibration is centered at 1454 cm−1. The strong peak at 1384 cm−1 belongs to the C–O vibration. The peak of 472 cm−1 is attributed to the Fe oxide.

The FT-IR spectrum of GQD-Fe3O4-Ru showed in Fig. 2 a, and the OH vibration appeared at 3420 cm−1, and the aliphatic C–H stretching vibration peak can be seen at 2925 cm−1. The peak at 1363 cm−1 decreased when the CO-Metal bond formed. The peak at 447 cm−1 belongs to Fe oxide.

The FT-IR spectrum of GQD-MgFe2O4 was enclosed in Fig. 2 b. The OH stretching vibration can be seen at 3423 cm−1, and the aliphatic C–H vibration presented at 2957, 2853 cm−1. The double bond stretching vibration centered at 1582 cm−1. The strong peak at 1382 cm−1 belongs to the C–O vibration that is produced when the citric acid is oxidized. The peak at 1052 cm−1 can be presented as an epoxy moiety. The peaks 836 and 439 cm−1 are to attributed metal oxide vibrations.

GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru was examined with the FT-IR technique, and the result showed that the O–H stretching vibration peak could be seen at 3422, and the aliphatic C-H stretching at 2923 cm−1. The peak at 1360 cm−1 is attributed to the C-O-Metal bond. The peak at 834, 479 cm−1 belongs to Mg and Fe oxide vibrations.

In the GQD-ZnFe2O4 Spectrum in Fig. 2 c, the OH stretching vibration can be seen at 3442 cm−1, and the aliphatic C–H vibration presented at 2920, 2850 cm−1. The appeared peak at 1624 cm−1 belongs to the CO stretching vibration that can be resulted from a carboxylic acid group in GQD. The double bond stretching vibration centered at 1577 cm−1. The strong peak at 1384 cm−1 belongs to the C-O vibration that is produced when the citric acid is oxidized. The peak at 1052 cm−1 can be presented as an epoxy moiety. The peak of 587 is attributed to the O-M-O (M=Zn, Fe).

With loading the Rutin to the GQD-ZnFe2O4, the aliphatic C–H stretching vibration peak increased and can be seen at 2923, 2854 cm−1. The O-H stretching vibration peak is sharper than the primary product peak when the O–Metal bond is produced. The peak at 1356 cm−1 decreased when the C-O-Metal bond formed. The peak at 805, and 610 cm−1 belongs to Zn and Fe oxygen bond.

The spectrum of GQD-CoFe2O4 is shown in Fig. 2 d. The peak at 3382 referred to OH stretching vibration, and the aliphatic C-H vibration can be seen at 2924, 2853 cm−1. The double bond stretching vibration centered at 1564 cm−1, and peaks 601, and 856 are attributed to the Co+2-O and Fe+3-O, respectively. The strong peak at 1356 cm−1 can be assigned to the C-O vibration that formed during the oxidation of Citric Acid to GQD and the observed peak at 1054 Cm−1 belong to the epoxied moiety.

In the spectrum of GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru, because of nanocomposite interaction with Rutin, the OH stretching presents at 3424 cm−1, and the cumulative aliphatic and the aromatic C-H vibration with high energy can be seen at 3024, and 2854 cm−1. The peak at 1625 cm−1 belongs to CO stretching vibration. The appeared peak at 598, 832 cm−1 attributed to the Co–O and Fe–O bonds. The vibration of the C–O bond appears in 1382 cm−1.

The spectrum of GQD-AgFe2O4 is shown in Fig. 2 e. The peak at 3420 cm−1 referred to OH stretching vibration, and the aliphatic C–H vibration can be seen at 2923, and 2853 cm−1. The peak at 1651 cm−1 belongs to the CO stretching vibration that can be resulted from a carboxylic acid group in GQD. The double bond stretching vibration centered at 1453 cm−1. The peak of 807 and 456 cm−1 was attributed to the Ag+2-O and Fe+3-O bond vibrations. The strong peak at 1356 cm−1 can be assigned to the C–O vibration that formed during the oxidation of citric acid to GQD and the observed peak at 1051 cm−1 belong to the epoxied moiety.

In the spectrum of GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru, because of nanocomposite interaction with Rutin, the OH stretching presents at 3421 cm−1, and the cumulative aliphatic and the aromatic C–H vibration with high energy can be seen at 2923, and 2853 cm−1. The peak at 1651 cm−1 belongs to CO stretching vibration. The peaks at 807 and 456 cm−1 are attributed to the Ag-O and Fe-O stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration of the C-O bond appears in 1356 cm−1.

The Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) study of as-prepared nanomagnetic samples was carried out at a temperature between 30 and 400 °C. The obtained data from the Fig. 3 thermograms, shows that endothermic reaction in the range of 80–100 °C could be attributed to the hydroxyl groups of graphene quantum dots that lost hydroxyl groups while the temperature raised. An exothermic reaction that appeared at 250–350 °C belongs to the dissociation of the metal oxide bond between metals and GQD and possible chelation between hydroxyl moiety of Rutin and metals. There was an endothermic reaction when Rutin was added to the GQD-MFe2O4. The peak in the 150–200 °C can be assigned to the melting point of Rutin that is incorporated into the GQD-MFe2O4 structure [7]. In Fig. 3 d, the sharp peak that appeared at 200 °C, demonstrates the melting temperature of GQD-ZnFe2O4.

Fig. 4A shows the XRD, EDX, and SEM images of GQD-Fe3O4. The X-ray diffraction of GQD-Fe3O4 in Fig. 4 (Ac) showed diffracted peaks observed at 2θ values of 31.8, 40.16, 41.52, 45.58, 56.6, 58.2, and 60.12. Similarly, the crystalline peak of GQD-Fe3O4-Ru occurred at 2θ values of 31.52, 32.68, 45.28, 45.32, 54.32, 57.8, and 61.16. These data approved the obtained results planes of Fe3O4 observed in the XRD spectra. Furthermore, results showed that there seemed to be a shift in the angles of the peaks [49].

The EDX analysis of GQD-Fe3O4 4(Ad) and GQD-Fe3O4-Rutin 4(Ae) show the O, C, and Fe elements. Appearing of O, C, and Fe elements in the EDX analysis confirmed the preparation of GQD-Fe3O4 and GQD-Fe3O4-Rutin. The insertion of Rutin in the GQD-Fe3O4 structure can be effected the high amount of O and C elements, confirmed by comparing the EDX GQD-Fe3O4 and GQD-Fe3O4-Rutin analysis.

The SEM image of GQD-ZnFe2O4 and GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru are shown in Fig. 4 B(a-b). Investigation of SEM image shows the agglomerated nanoparticles with diameters of less than 20 nanometers. These nanoparticles are possibly related to the zinc-iron oxide nanoparticles that formed on the graphene quantum dot surface.

Fig. 4(Bc) shows the diffraction pattern of GQD-ZnFe2O4 and GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru. The peaks at 2θ values of GQD-ZnFe2O4 were observed at 29.52, 31.88, 36.36, 42.84, 45.6, 56.56, and 66, respectively. The synthesized GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru X-ray diffraction analysis showed 2θ at 31.68, 45.32, 54.08, 59.64, and 65.2 values. The intensity of peaks for the GQD-ZnFe2O4 is higher than the GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru [50].

Results from the study of the EDX pattern of GQD-ZnFe2O4 in Fig. 4 (Bd) revealed that Zn, Fe, C, and O elements are present in the GQD-ZnFe2O4 composition. Also, the EDX pattern of GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru in Fig. 4 (Be) confirmed the presence of Zn, Fe, C, and O elements. The difference between GQD-ZnFe2O4, and GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru is the amount of O and C in the nanocomposite structure.

The SEM image of GQD-MgFe2O4 and GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru are depicted in Fig. 4 C(a-b). As can be seen, there are agglomerated nanoparticles with diameters of less than 20 nanometers. These nanoparticles can be attributed to the magnesium-iron oxide nanoparticles.

The X-Ray investigation of GQD-MgFe2O4 and GQD-MgFe2O4 -Ru in Fig. 4 (Cc), show the 2θ peaks at 23, 27.48, 29.48, 31.84, 35.52, 38.96, 42.6, 46.56, 47.26, 50.52, 60.6 and 66.32 and so at 27.32, 29.4, 31.72, 38.2, 42.48, 45.44, 48 and 56.50, respectively. A comparison of the intensity and disappearance of peaks in these patterns shows that there is a significant difference between their crystallinity [51,52].

The EDX pattern of GQD-MgFe2O4 (Fig. 4 (Cd)) includes Mg, Fe, C, and O. The presence of Mg confirmed its successful synthesis of it. The EDX pattern of GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru (Fig. 4 (Ce)) shows the elements of the Mg, Fe, C, and O. The high amount of C and O elements contributed to the inclusion of Rutin in the nanocomposite.

The crystallinity of nano metal composite was evaluated by X-ray diffraction, in Fig. 4 (Dc). The X-ray pattern of GQD-CoFe2O4 shows the peaks of 2θ at 30.4, 32.76, 35.76, 43.52, 53.96, 57.44, and 63.12, also, the peaks of 2θ GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru were observed in 30.44, 32.72, 43.4, 53.96, 57.32, and 62.96. The results showed no meaningful differences between GQD-CoFe2O4 and GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru crystallinity [53].

The obtained results from the EDX pattern of GQD-CoFe2O4 (Fig. 4 (Dd)) revealed that Co, Fe, C, and O elements are included in the GQD-CoFe2O4 composition. The EDX pattern of GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru (Fig. 4 (Ae)) shows that the GQD-CoFe2O4 and GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru bear the Co, Fe, C, and O elements. As expected, the amounts of O and C are more in GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru, related to the incorporation of Rutin in the nanocomposite structure.

The SEM images of GQD-CoFe2O4 and GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru in Fig. 4 C(a-b), show the nanoparticles with diameters of less than 20 nanometers. These cobalt-iron oxide nanoparticles are formed on the graphene quantum dot surface.

The SEM images of GQD-AgFe2O4 and GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru (Fig. 4 E(a-b)) show porous structures containing nanowire and nanoparticles of silver-iron oxide on the quantum dot graphene surface. The diameter of nanowires is about 60 nanometers and the diameter of silver-iron oxides is less than 20 nanometers.

In Fig. 4 (Ec), the X-Ray pattern of GQD-AgFe2O4 and GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru, depict the peaks of 2θ at 21.4, 27.92, 32.32, 32.44, 46.16, 46.28, 54.92, 57.52, and 67.64, and so the peaks of 2θ at 21.32, 27.48, 38.24, 44.52, 45.56, 56.56, and 64.48, respectively. There are many differences between GQD-AgFe2O4 and GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru X-ray patterns. The appearance of new peaks at 38.24, and 56.56, and combination of two peaks at 54.92 and 57.52, and the change in the intensity and the number of peaks are major differences, so we can say, these two combinations have a completely different crystal structures [51–54].

The EDX patterns of GQD-AgFe2O4 and GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru (Fig. 4 E(d-e)) show the Ag, Fe, C, and O elements and incorporation of Ag to the GQD-Fe3O4 nanocomposite structure, also the high amounts of C and O elements that can be related to the addition of Rutin moiety to the nanocomposite in GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru.

The UV-Visible studies were used to investigate as-prepared nanomagnetic composites at Ethanol-Water solvent, and the spectra were represented in Fig. 5. The absorption peak at about 350–365 nm could be assigned to the O-dihydroxyl benzoyl system and the absorption peak at about 260–265 nm almost belong to the M- dihydroxyl benzoyl system of Rutin [55].
The Rutin UV-Visible spectra showed the maximum absorption at 271 nm and 373 nm. The obtained results represent the blue shift in the UV-Visible spectra of prepared nanomagnetic composites.

The voltammogram of Rutin (0.1 mM) has been recorded in acetonitrile solvent in the presence of 0.1 M lithium perchlorate counter ion with a glassy carbon electrode as a working electrode. Platinum rode electrode as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode at different potential scan rates of 25–200 mV s−1 in the potential window of 0–1 V vs. SCE as represented in Fig. 6. This redox reaction includes the reduction and oxidation of lithium ions on the surface of the glassy carbon, as given in the figure below. As can be seen, there is an oxidation peak at about 0.7 and a reduction peak at about 0.3 V.

The Rutin shows the quasi-reversible behavior in the acetonitrile and lithium perchlorate. Also, the anodic and cathodic peak currents increased while increasing the scan rate. As a result, a linear relationship confirms that redox reactions in the system are kinetically fast diffusion-controlled reactions.

The electrochemical performance of metal oxide-magnetic graphene quantum dot composite was carried out at different potential scan rates of 25–200 mV s−1 in the potential window of 0–1 V vs. SCE. The cyclic voltammogram of GQD-Fe3O4, GQD-Fe3O4-Ru, GQD-MgFe2O4, and GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru is depicted in Fig. 6 (a-d). The cyclic voltammograms of GQD-Fe3O4 (Fig. 6 (a)) show oxidation peaks at 0.3 and 0.7 V and reduction peaks at 0.3 V. Considering the Rutin voltammogram, the CV results of the metal oxide nano-magnetic composites in the graphene quantum dot matrices in the presence of Rutin are not the exception. With the addition of Rutin to the GQD-Fe3O4, the CV plot of GQD-Fe3O4-Ru 6(b) intensified. The CV plot of GQD-Fe3O4-Ru shows oxidation peaks at 0.3 and 0.7 V and reduction peaks at 0.2 V.

The CV plot of GQD-MgFe2O4 and GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru is depicted in Fig. 6 (c-d). There are oxidation peaks at 0.4, and 0.8 V and reduction peaks at 0.2 V. The oxidation peaks for GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru were observed at 0.7 V, and the reduction peaks at 0.2 V. Incorporation of Rutin in the GQD-MgFe2O4 composite intensified the CV plot of GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru compare to the GQD-MgFe2O4.

The CV plot of GQD-ZnFe2O4, GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru, GQD-CoFe2O4, and GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru are depicted in Fig. 6 (g-j). The CV plot for the GQD-ZnFe2O4 showed the oxidation peaks at 0.3 and 0.8 V and reduction peaks at 0.3 V (Fig. 6 (g)), and the CV plot of GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru showed oxidation peaks at 0.4, and 0.8 V, and reduction peaks at 0.2 V (Fig. 6 (h)).
The overview of GQD-CoFe2O4 and GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru revealed oxidation peaks at 0.2 and 0.8 V and reduction peaks at 0.3 V, respectively (Fig. 6 (i-j))
The survey of GQD-AgFe2O4 and GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru show the oxidation peaks at 0.2 and 0.7 V and reduction peaks at 0.2 and 0.1 V, respectively (Fig. 6 (e-f))

The graphene quantum dots are electroactive materials that could distribute the charge flow on the surface coming from the redox reaction on metal and metal oxides in the nanomagnetic composite. It might lead to the widening of the peaks in the cyclic voltammogram. However, adding of the Rutin could adjust this pattern and make the reaction exclusive to a specific range of potential. This intensified pattern is observed in the as-synthesized nanomagnetic composites, and the increasing CV plot area confirmed the incorporation of organic Rutin into the nanomagnetic composite structure.

The antioxidant performance of the prepared metal nanocomposite was measured by the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhidrazyl (DPPH) radical scavenging method. The new DPPH solution was prepared and used under light protection conditions. The alcoholic solution was mixed with the metal nanocomposite dissolved in ethanol 96%. Light absorbance of samples was tested at a wavelength of 517 nm using a spectrophotometer. The absorbances were recorded after one h in a stabilized condition and the result is represented in Fig. 7 a. The results show a decreased value in absorbance.

The RSA% was calculated by Eq. (1) and the results are represented in Fig. 7 b.
(1) %RSA = [(A0-A1)/A0] × 100 when A0 is 0.1399·
The results show that the antioxidant activity is as below:
GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru<GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru<Rutin<GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru<GQD-Fe3O4-Ru< GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru.

The coordination of Rutin with Zr, Mo, Zn, Cu, Ni, Fe, and the antioxidant activity of the Rutin-Metal complex reported by Om Prakash and co-workers. The obtained results show the antioxidant activity between 72-84% inhibitions of DPPH test at 500 µg/mL concentration [56]. In a study by Sung Min Pyro’s group the rutin nanocrystal gel were prepared and the antioxidant activity of nanocrystal show a highest activity 90% [57].

In summary, we synthesized some Rutin-Metal magnetic graphene quantum dots nanocomposites by thermal pyrolysis. The magnetic nanocomposites were confirmed by FT-IR, SEM, XRD, EDX, DSC, UV-Visible, and CV techniques. The evaluation of the SEM images shows the nanostructure at about 20 nm. The CV performance of nanocomposites was carried out at different potential scan rates of 25–200 mV s−1 in the potential window of 0–1 V vs. SCE. The results of CV plots show quasi-reversible properties for the synthesized nanocomposites. The DPPH studies of nanocomposites show different antioxidant capacities. The magnetic nanocomposites have exhibited antioxidant properties compared to Rutin as GQD-ZnFe2O4-Ru<GQD-CoFe2O4-Ru<Rutin<GQD-MgFe2O4-Ru<GQD-Fe3O4-Ru< GQD-AgFe2O4-Ru.

The magnetic quantum dot graphene Rutin derivatives with increased antioxidant activity and penetration can be used in new drug derivatives for drug delivery with potential biological and free radical scavenger properties [58].

Dear Dr. Davide Prosperi,Thank you for your e-mail regarding my manuscript (No.: COLSUB-D-22–01508) titled Preparation and characterization of Rutin loaded on magnetic graphene quantum dot nano career with metals (Ag, Zn, Mg, Co, and Fe) and study of antioxidant and electrochemical properties. The reviewer’s comments were made in full in the article and sent., With best regards, Dr. Abdolreza Abri, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University.
